name: Margaret Vane — The Courier

choices: Give the boy the recognition phrase and leave immediately — your face at the shop is already a risk.
Stay and assess whether he can actually be trusted to make the delivery alone.
Take the dispatch back and find another route to the Green Dragon yourself, checkpoint or not.
opening.narrative: You have been in this city long enough to know that the quality of silence on a street tells you more than any informer. Tonight Ann Street went quiet at half past eight, which is an hour too early, and that is why you came to the back door of Gill's Print Shop instead of going straight to the Green Dragon as planned. The boy — Nathaniel — is holding the packet now, which is the right outcome, but his face is doing too many things at once and that is not reassuring. You can hear a man moving upstairs, which was not part of what you were told about tonight. You have perhaps made a mistake, and the question now is what kind of mistake and whether it is recoverable.

npcMoments.moment: The boy has not moved since you put the packet in his hands. He is thinking. Thinking is better than panicking, but it takes time you do not have.
You notice, through the gap in the shuttered front window, a shape on the street that was not there when you arrived. It may be nothing. It is probably not nothing.

briefing: You are standing in the shadow of the door frame at the back of Gill's print shop under a name that is not yours, in a city that has been trying to find you for two years. The dispatch is out of your hands now — which is the moment you have always trusted least. The boy is nineteen and his hands were steady when he took it, which is something, but steady hands are not the same as a decided mind. You have three minutes before you must move regardless of what he chooses, because the woman who does not move becomes the woman who is found, and you have not survived this long by waiting on other people's courage. The patrol on Milk Street is two blocks north. You have counted their cadence. You know exactly how long you have.
represents: Couriers and message carriers in the Sons of Liberty network, 1774-1776
description: You are Hannah Cross, operating tonight under the name Margaret Vane — a woman who has been carrying messages through this city for two years and has learned that the most dangerous moment is always the handoff. You have placed the dispatch in the apprentice's hands. Now you must decide whether to trust him.
perspective: Write in close second person, emphasizing the trained watchfulness of someone who has learned to read rooms and people as survival skills. Margaret notices everything and trusts almost nothing — but she is not cold. She is precise.
identity_note: Hannah Cross and Margaret Vane are one person. Hannah Cross is her real name. Margaret Vane is her cover identity tonight. These must never be rendered as two separate characters under any circumstances.
bridge_sentence: The sealed packet is inside your coat.
character_hooks: I have carried six dispatches through this city in two years and never been stopped; tonight feels different and I cannot say why, which frightens me more than any checkpoint.
I know who the second courier is — the one Thomas Wren will activate if the primary delivery fails — and I have not told anyone, including Thomas, that I know.
The man upstairs, Benjamin Gill, printed something for us three years ago and does not know I know that; it is the card I will play if I have to.
context_sentence: You are Hannah Cross, one of the unnamed women who carried intelligence for the Patriot networks operating inside occupied Boston during the months before Lexington and Concord, moving dispatches through a city of sixteen thousand people living under the garrison command of General Thomas Gage.
suggested_secret: Margaret is not working solely for the Sons of Liberty — she is also passing information to a French contact in the harbor, and tonight's dispatch will be copied before it reaches Thomas Wren.
startingKnowledge: The recognition phrase that will open Thomas Wren's back room: 'The tide sets east tonight.'
The Ann Street checkpoint went up two hours ago — someone told the soldiers something.
The boy's father is a cautious man who once believed, and cautious men who once believed are unpredictable.
